schools.summary: Keil sets the scene (Jezreel a summer seat); Ellicott weighs the vexed question of whether the vineyard lay at Jezreel or Samaria.
Gill dates the coveting after the Syrian wars in which Ahab spared Benhadad the blasphemer.
Henry: possessions become a snare; inordinate desire, even by lawful means, breaks the tenth commandment.

debates.question: Where did Naboth's vineyard and the crime lie — Jezreel or Samaria?

sides.position: near Samaria, on the whole the more probable
at Jezreel, as the received text and 'his city' imply

schools.summary: Keil: Naboth was bound by God's command, not merely free to refuse; Ellicott finds the story 'true to nature,' the refusal right, Ahab a spoilt child.
Clarke: the offer seemed fair, but Ahab sought a final alienation the law forbade; even despots dared not seize inherited land.
Gill: the inheritance was not to be sold away from the family.

themes: a lawful request, an unlawful aim

schools.summary: Gill: Naboth, under no necessity to sell, refuses out of regard to God's law and his family, a conscientious man of the seven thousand.
Henry: some think he held his earthly inheritance an earnest of the heavenly Canaan; he would rather risk the king's displeasure than offend God.
Keil: a childish sulk that exposes a man 'sold under sin' who lacks only the energy to act his wickedness.
Clarke: lord of ten-twelfths of the land yet miserable — the soul without God has no happiness.
Henry: discontent is its own punishment and its own parent, arising not from the condition but from the mind.
Gill: Ahab returns in the same heavy humour he had shown after the prophet's earlier rebuke.
Henry: under pretence of comfort Jezebel feeds Ahab's pride; her 'Dost thou govern Israel?' is bent to counsel tyranny.
Keil hears an ironical taunt — the emphatic 'thou' scorning a king too timid to act.
Gill: she perceives his sadness and means to relieve him.
Henry: Ahab conceals Naboth's conscientious reason, reporting a righteous 'I may not' as a stubborn 'I will not.'
Keil: the concealment prepares the forged, sealed letters that lend a private malice the crown's authority.
Gill: Ahab had offered to buy the vineyard at Naboth's own price.

themes: God's name left out of the story

schools.summary: Ellicott: her scorn is like Lady Macbeth's, despising a self-indulgent king fit only to desire and revel.
Clarke: 'Naboth, not Ahab, is king' — the vital language of despotism and tyranny.
Gill: the tempter's flattery that a king's will is its own law over so trifling a matter.
Ellicott: the seal was the pledge of authority, and its use implies Ahab knew a deed was done in his name he took care not to inquire into.
Gill: he gave leave to use his seal, though she kept her scheme from him.
Clarke: she counterfeited his authority by his own consent, and he lent his signet.
Ellicott: the fast is a religious cloak, implying a secret sin to be purged and marking a victim; 'set on high' is ambiguous — honour or arraignment.
Gill: a pretended fear of calamity; Kimchi reads 'convene a court'; why only Jezreel if it were a true public fast?
Clarke: intimate a calamity for some tolerated evil, and 'set Naboth on high' to bring him to public trial.
Henry: Naboth is to be fastened on as the suspect, perhaps for not joining his neighbours' worship.
Ellicott: two witnesses per the law; the charge-word properly means 'to bless,' hence 'renounce' rather than 'blaspheme,' yet stoning followed as for blasphemy.
Clarke: two were needed to attaint life; 'blaspheme God and the king' means atheist and rebel; he prefers 'curse' over Parkhurst's idolatry reading.
Gill: sons of Belial, lawless men who will swear anything, set to accuse Naboth to his face.
Henry: a 'complicated blasphemy' — the God-charge forfeits life, the king-charge forfeits the estate Ahab wants.

debates.question: What does the accusation word (barach) mean here?

sides.position: a euphemism for cursing / blaspheming God and the king
'to renounce / disown' rather than to blaspheme

schools.summary: Ellicott: the ready acquiescence is the mark of Eastern despotism that poisons justice under form of law, the worse where the king is God's vicegerent under the supreme law; Keil calls it deep corruption and slavish fear.
Gill: astonishing that the chief magistrates were so depraved — idolatry takes away all sense of humanity and justice.
Keil: the elders executed the command without delay, proof of deep corruption and slavish fear.
Gill: 'proclaimed a fast' is to be read as 'called a court.'
Henry: no wickedness is so vile but religion has been made a cover for it — yet the ordinance is not the worse for the abuse.
Ellicott: Naboth is carried outside the walls, as usual, to avoid polluting the city with blood — possibly to his own coveted ground.
Clarke: the whole family was likely destroyed and the grounds confiscated ('the blood of his sons').
Gill: the two accusers sit facing the accused, Roman-fashion; Josephus says three, the versions two.
Henry: the sons stoned too, that none might claim the estate; God will make inquisition for blood.
Gill: the report goes to Jezebel, who held the sway at court and would welcome the death of one of the seven thousand.
Henry: the murder is sent as 'agreeable news'; the same elders who kill on command will one day serve Jehu's slaughter of Ahab's house.
Keil and Ellicott: with the heirs dead the estate is confiscated on the principle of high treason, though no law prescribed it; Elijah's question is framed to sharpen Ahab's conscience.
Clarke: by what rites the possession was taken, we do not know.
Henry: Ahab goes down in state to claim the plunder; might prevails against right, and wonderful is the divine patience.
Ellicott: Ahab asks no question, wishing to be innocent of the knowledge yet to applaud the deed; the Septuagint's sackcloth here is a likely interpolation from v27.
Gill: informed by Jezebel, Ahab rose up to go down and take possession.
Ellicott: Elijah, long in solitude and absent from the political service, emerges for the higher duty of rebuking crime and avenging innocent blood.
Henry: the father of the prophets is sent to the vineyard at the hour of possession — 'then and there must his doom be read.'
Gill: perhaps Elijah had spent the interval reviving the schools of the prophets.

themes: judgment at the scene of the crime

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'which is in Samaria' is meaningless unless it means Ahab was literally there — a key note in the geography question; Clarke dates the meeting the day after the murder.
Gill: it denotes Ahab's palace and seat at Samaria (some connect it with his title over Israel).
Clarke: 'the next day after the murder,' as the later narrative shows.

debates.question: Does 'which is in Samaria' fix Ahab's location or merely give his title?

sides.position: Ahab was literally in Samaria, not Jezreel
it is part of his royal title / the country

schools.summary: Ellicott: the emphatic 'even thine' precludes transferring the fulfilment to Jehoram, and v29 does not defer this clause.
Clarke: no literal fulfilment on Ahab in person — the dogs licked his blood at the pool of Samaria, and Jehoram's body fell in Naboth's field.
Gill: the murder is laid on Ahab, whose covetousness caused it and who abetted it by taking possession.

debates.question: How and where was 'dogs shall lick thy blood' fulfilled?

sides.position: deferred — at the pool of Samaria and in Jehoram at Jezreel
on Ahab himself, the emphatic 'even thine' forbidding transfer

schools.summary: Ellicott: Ahab's cry is dismay and excuse, attributing rebuke to mere enmity; the crushing answer is that he had 'sold himself,' a slave not a king.
Henry: Ahab both hates and fears the prophet, the sight of him like the writing on the wall to Belshazzar.
Clarke: 'sold thyself to work evil' — Satan his absolute master, he the undivided slave.
Gill: Ahab reckons Elijah his enemy for faithful reproof.

themes: the prophet as the true friend

schools.summary: Keil: the LORD will take away Ahab's posterity, cutting off every male and making his house like Jeroboam's and Baasha's for the provocation.
Gill: the evil falls first on Ahab's own person by a violent death, though he has seventy sons.

themes: the sin recoiling on the house

schools.summary: Keil: the ground of the doom is the standing charge of provoking God and making Israel to sin, which felled Jeroboam and Baasha before.
Gill: two predecessors whose families were utterly destroyed, in whose line Ahab is now enrolled.
Ellicott: only now does Elijah name 'the bolder criminal,' pronouncing on Jezebel a doom of shame — her body cast as offal to the scavenger dogs.
Clarke: most literally fulfilled, as the carcasses of the punished are thrown to dogs and vultures.
Gill: the LORD spoke of Jezebel too — the dogs shall eat her by the wall of Jezreel.
Gill: 'they shall have no burial'; here the words of the LORD by Elijah end and the historian's own reflection begins.
Ellicott: the compiler's verdict, catching Elijah's word, is condemnation and contempt for 'a king most unkingly,' selling himself for a paltry desire.
Clarke: 'he hired himself to the devil'; a bad wife is from the devil, and Jezebel has had many successors.
Gill: none like Ahab, worse even than the predecessors whose houses were destroyed.
Henry: he 'sold himself,' voluntary where Paul was 'sold under sin' against his will, taking sin's wages, death.
Ellicott points to the 'nameless abominations' of idol-worship; Keil marks the turn, calling Ahab's coming repentance sincere though not lasting.
Gill: he followed idols — 'dunghill gods' — after the manner of the cast-out Amorites.
Henry: worst of all his sins was following idols, more provoking to God than his immoralities.
Clarke: the sorrow was unfeigned, and God's approbation puts its genuineness beyond doubt; 'went softly' most likely means barefooted.
Ellicott and Keil read the humiliation as real if not lasting.
Henry: the garments rent but not the heart — an external show that soon gave way to fresh hatred of a prophet.

debates.question: Was Ahab's repentance genuine or merely outward?

sides.position: genuine and sincere, accepted by God
external only, the heart unhumbled and unchanged

schools.summary: Keil: the LORD notes Ahab's self-humbling and defers the evil to his son's days.
Gill: the word came after Ahab had been some time humbled, again 'the word of prophecy.'
Gill: the humiliation was external only, born of dread — he never reproved Jezebel, restored the vineyard, or left his idols — yet it was noticed by the LORD.
Clarke: he did truly repent, genuine in God's sight; the penitent heart ever meets the merciful eye of God.
Ellicott: though it might seem the offspring of fear, God sees in its imperfection 'some germs of promise' and remits part of the penalty.
Henry: goodness rejoicing against judgment — mark the good even in the not-so-good; the reprieve foreshadows the justifying of the sincere.
Gill reports that Luther, pondering the verse, was persuaded to a more hopeful verdict on Ahab than the narrative will bear.

debates.question: Was Ahab's accepted humbling genuine repentance or the offspring of fear?

sides.position: mere external humiliation, arising from dread of punishment
true repentance meeting the merciful eye of God
imperfect and fear-tinged, yet holding 'germs of promise' and not disregarded

outliers.note: Gill records that Luther drew from this verse a hopeful conclusion in Ahab's favour.
